sources.notes: Find the minimum common increment making every a_i special, where special means f_6(x) > f_7(x).
Official solution uses the recursive rule by divisibility by 42.
Cross-check for enumerating answer modulo 42 and recursing on the divisible-by-42 class.

annotation.summary: Given an array, find the smallest t such that every a_i + t has more factors of 6 than factors of 7, or report impossible.
annotation.constraints: Total n <= 2e5 and a_i <= 1e9; the solution must avoid searching t directly and recurse through base-42 residue classes.
annotation.core_idea: If 42 does not divide x, then x is special exactly when x mod 42 is a positive multiple of 6. If 42 divides x, both f_6 and f_7 decrease by one after dividing by 42, so specialness is inherited by x/42. Enumerate t modulo 42. Residues that cannot make every value a multiple of 6 are impossible; for the unique residue class that becomes divisible by 42, divide shifted values by 42 and solve recursively.
annotation.complexity: O(n log_42 A) up to the constant factor from checking 42 residues per recursion level.
tricks: only recurse on the class divisible by 42

solution_variants.name: mod42-recursive-residue-split
solution_variants.summary: Enumerate the increment modulo 42, discard already-special residue classes, and recurse only on shifted values divisible by 42.

tags.evidence: The core criterion is expressed through divisibility exponents of 6, 7, and 42.
The algorithm enumerates t modulo 42 and uses residues modulo 6 and 7 to decide immediate specialness.
After fixing t mod 42, solved residue classes are discarded and the divisible-by-42 class is divided by 42 and solved recursively.
Large values and unbounded t are reduced to repeated residue decisions and quotient values after division by 42.
